db-001.title: Why Rhon Studios: Building a Game Studio from Scratch
db-001.excerpt: The story behind the decision to found an indie game studio, the doubts we faced at the beginning, and why we believe it's worth pursuing.
db-001.content:
**Starting a game studio from scratch is one of the most irrational decisions you can make.** No external funding, no safety net, no guarantees. Just the conviction that you have **something to say**, and that video games are the medium through which to say it.

Rhon Studios was born from that conviction. **Not from a business plan,** but from a long conversation about the kind of experiences we want to create and why there still isn't anything that **fully satisfies us as players.**

In this first devblog entry, we want to be honest about where we are: a small studio, developing original IPs, with no revenue yet, but with a clear direction and the determination to see it through. **This is the beginning. And we want you to experience it with us.**

db-002.title: Revenue Share at Rhon Studios: Transparency & Collaboration
db-002.excerpt: A deep dive into how our revenue share model works, the legal framework behind indie videogame development in Spain, and our commitment to a fair and transparent system for all collaborators.
db-002.content:
One of the biggest challenges when starting an indie game studio from scratch is finding a way to fairly compensate the talent that helps bring the vision to life before external funding arrives. At Rhon Studios, we address this through a structured **Revenue Share** model. Operating under Spanish law, specifically relying on the freedom of contract established by **Article 1255 of the Spanish Civil Code**, this framework defines a civil collaboration without an employment relationship, where both risk and success are shared mutually. 

The net profits generated by our projects are distributed transparently: 

 - **70% is allocated to the pool of collaborators.**

 - **30% is reinvested into the studio to sustain development.** 

The individual percentages within that 70% pool are determined based on the **actual quantity, quality, and complexity of the work delivered**. We establish operational measures such as a minimum payout threshold of €10 to avoid administrative friction, and payments are initially made on a monthly basis to support the team. However, to keep our model realistic and ensure long-term sustainability, our terms include **specific limits**.

The right to receive these payments is tied to the project's actual **commercial lifespan**; for example, the maximum total compensation is capped at the equivalent of the net profits generated during the first full year of commercial exploitation, ensuring that rewards accurately reflect the game's **true maximum commercial performance**. 

Our goal is to build an ecosystem based on **trust and professional growth**, giving everyone a share of what we create **together**.

db-003.title: Afterlight’s light and shadow system: how it started as a sketch
db-003.excerpt:
When we started designing Afterlight, we had one idea written down before any mechanic existed: two characters who help each other even though they belong to worlds that hurt one another. One of light, one of shadow. One strong where the other is weak.

The design question came afterwards: how do we make that be felt, instead of just told?
db-003.content:
**The first version didn’t work**

At first, light and shadow were only aesthetic. The player, Lumen, moved through areas that were more or less dark, and the lighting changed the atmosphere, but it didn’t change anything about how the game was played. It looked atmospheric. But while testing it, we realized there was a problem: if Lumen moved just as well in darkness as in light, the entire premise of the story — that light and shadow are opposing forces that weaken each other — wasn’t being felt through the player’s hands, it was only being read in a text.

That was the first real lesson of development: if the mechanic didn’t reflect the relationship between Lumen and Umbra, it wasn’t a mechanic, it was decoration.

**Going back to the sketch: light as a force, not a filter**

So we did something that was difficult for us to accept at the time: we let Lumen literally feel weaker the farther away he moved from the light. Not just visually — slower, with heavier animations, with less room to react during combat. The divided area where our world exists (the light zone, the shadow zone, and the middle zone where neither of the two races can fully live) stopped being just a setting and became the character’s own health bar.

And so that this change in state wouldn’t just be an abstract debuff, we added the streetlights: small sources of light scattered throughout the level that the player can activate to recover strength. The first time we tested this, in a very early level where Lumen wakes up weakened after a collapse and has to turn on a single streetlight to be able to continue moving forward, we realized it worked: turning on that light no longer felt like a tutorial task, it felt like a small physical victory.

**The twist that gave everything meaning**

The real change came when we decided to reverse the rule as the player progresses. At the beginning, Lumen has total control: he moves with ease, the light strengthens him, and a weak, trapped silhouette, Umbra, can barely keep up with him. But the further the journey goes into shadow territory, the more that relationship reverses: Lumen loses agility and strength, while Umbra regains it.

We don’t explain it through dialogue. We explain it through the characters’ own movement. And there is a very specific moment in the level design where this becomes confirmed as a gameplay mechanic, not just background narrative: at a certain point, Lumen cannot move forward alone because an area is covered in dense darkness that hurts him just by touching it. The only way to continue is for Umbra to absorb that darkness for him. The first time we tested it in the prototype, with programmer art and zero visual polish, it already felt like the heart of the game: your companion protects you from what would hurt you, and vice versa.

**Where it is now**

Right now, we are fine-tuning how this state change is represented throughout the game’s four zones, from the completely illuminated area where Lumen begins, to the absolute shadow territory that Umbra belongs to, without it feeling like a numerical health bar, but rather like something that can be seen and felt in every jump, every hit, every extra second it takes for a character to react.

We are still iterating. Every level test makes us adjust the pacing of this reversal a little, and we would rather make it feel right than rush to have it finished.

We’ll leave you with a real question we’ve been asking ourselves internally this week:

**Do you think a player should notice earlier that something is going to change, or do you prefer the surprise of losing strength to catch you as you progress, without warning?**

We’re reading everything you share with us. This is still being built, and your ideas genuinely help us.

db-004.title: Designing an enemy that IS the darkness, not just reskinned as one
db-004.excerpt: Last week we told you how light and shadow became a real mechanic. This week comes the next obvious question: if the world is split between light and shadow, who lives in between, where neither rule fully applies?
db-004.content:
**The problem with "generic" enemies**

At first we tried enemies that were, basically, dark-colored versions of normal monsters: something to attack, something to dodge, with no real connection to the game's central theme beyond spawning in a dark area. They worked as obstacles, but added nothing to the story we're trying to tell. And since Afterlight leans heavily on telling things without dialogue, an enemy that communicates nothing about the world is a missed opportunity.

**The idea: beings with no side, no eyes, no conscience**

That's how what we internally call "The Extinguished" (Los Apagados) was born: beings that belong to neither the race of light nor the race of shadow. They have no ideals, no allegiance, only one motivation, survival, and to that end they attack anyone who enters their zone. We deliberately designed them without eyes, a visual detail that in our world marks the difference between "civilized" beings (light, shadow) and those that aren't. It's a silent way of telling the player, with zero text, that this isn't someone you can reason with.

**The real challenge: making the AI respect the world's rule**

This is where the design got interesting. Since we'd already established that light and shadow affect Lumen and Umbra's physical strength, it made no sense for enemies to ignore that same rule. So these beings' behavior also depends on which zone they're in: they don't react the same way in a brightly lit area as in dense shadow, and how they chase or retreat changes based on the light balance of the environment, the very same system that already affects the protagonists.

The first time we tested this with a simple enemy, with no final art, it became clear this multiplied the meaning of combat: it's not just "dodge and hit," it's understanding what terrain you're standing on and what advantage or disadvantage that gives you.

**Where it stands now**

We're still tuning this AI's behavior states: how much it should chase, when it should retreat, how it should react near a lit lamppost. We want it to feel coherent with the rest of the world, not like a separate enemy bolted on.

A real question for this week:

**Would you rather have enemies behave predictably once you learn their pattern, or have some variation so you're never fully sure how they'll react?**

As always, we read everything you tell us.
